coredata.dc:description: The employment of photoluminescent carbon nanodots (PL-CNDs) in biomedical applications is a new trend of research, specifically in delivering and tracking the drug of interest at the target site. On the other hand, the determination of in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility of the highly utilized nanomaterial is of utmost priority. In the present work, we described the synthesis, optical and surface morphological characterization, and determination of toxicity of PL-CNDs derived from egg white on the in vitro Human Corneal Epithelial (HCE) cells, and in vivo Drosophila melanogaster (D. melanogaster) model. The in-house developed PL-CNDs were blue photoluminescent under excitation at 365 nm and showed an emission peak at 420 nm. Further, HR-TEM analysis suggests the spherical shape of PL-CNDs with a lattice space of 0.26 nm, having a size of 2 nm determined from particle size analysis. The Tauc plot analysis calculated from absorption spectra suggests a band gap of ∼1.8 eV and ∼4.9 eV. The biocompatibility assay (20–250 μg/mL), cell apoptosis (20, 60, 100 μg/mL), and cell morphological (20, 60, 100 μg/mL) studies on HCE cells highlight the cytocompatibility of the PL-CNDs at various concentrations. The toxicity of PL-CNDs was evaluated using an in vivo genetic animal model, D. melanogaster. The oral treatment of 20–250 μg/mL concentrations of PL-CNDs did not induce any significant mortality and climbing defect in flies. In addition, dietary supplementation with PL-CNDs elicits a dose-dependent enhancement of antioxidant defense mechanisms confirmed by SOD and CAT, and the elevation of total antioxidant capacity. The measurement of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) in the brain and the gastrointestinal (GI) tract region suggests the non-toxic properties of PL-CNDs (50–100 μg/mL). In summary, PL-CNDs are non-toxic and have good bioavailability, they can be used for delivering drugs in conjugations due to their nano size.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The employment of photoluminescent carbon nanodots (PL-CNDs) in biomedical applications is a new trend of research, specifically in delivering and tracking the drug of interest at the target site. On the other hand, the determination of in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility of the highly utilized nanomaterial is of utmost priority. In the present work, we described the synthesis, optical and surface morphological characterization, and determination of toxicity of PL-CNDs derived from egg white on the in vitro Human Corneal Epithelial (HCE) cells, and in vivo Drosophila melanogaster (D. melanogaster) model. The in-house developed PL-CNDs were blue photoluminescent under excitation at 365 nm and showed an emission peak at 420 nm. Further, HR-TEM analysis suggests the spherical shape of PL-CNDs with a lattice space of 0.26 nm, having a size of 2 nm determined from particle size analysis. The Tauc plot analysis calculated from absorption spectra suggests a band gap of ∼1.8 eV and ∼4.9 eV. The biocompatibility assay (20–250 μg/mL), cell apoptosis (20, 60, 100 μg/mL), and cell morphological (20, 60, 100 μg/mL) studies on HCE cells highlight the cytocompatibility of the PL-CNDs at various concentrations. The toxicity of PL-CNDs was evaluated using an in vivo genetic animal model, D. melanogaster. The oral treatment of 20–250 μg/mL concentrations of PL-CNDs did not induce any significant mortality and climbing defect in flies. In addition, dietary supplementation with PL-CNDs elicits a dose-dependent enhancement of antioxidant defense mechanisms confirmed by SOD and CAT, and the elevation of total antioxidant capacity. The measurement of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) in the brain and the gastrointestinal (GI) tract region suggests the non-toxic properties of PL-CNDs (50–100 μg/mL). In summary, PL-CNDs are non-toxic and have good bioavailability, they can be used for delivering drugs in conjugations due to their nano size.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are ultra-nanosized carbonaceous materials that demonstrate high photoluminescence, good in vitro and in vivo compatibility, water solubility, photostability, and multi-functionalization ability [1,2]. Moreover, the high quantum yield (QY), environmentally friendly characteristics, chemical stability, advantageous surface-to-volume ratio, biocompatibility, and economic feasibility provide a remarkable opportunity for photoluminescent carbon nanodots (PL-CNDs) to be utilized across diverse fields. As a result, PL-CNDs have been extensively used in a range of applications such as drug and gene delivery, sensing, bioimaging, optoelectronics, and catalysis [3–5]. PL-CNDs can be produced through two different strategies: the ‘top-down’ approach and the ‘bottom-up’ approach. The top-down approach involves decomposing a large molecule into its nanomaterial form, while the bottom-up approach refers to the creation of PL nanomaterials by combining two specific organic precursors under controlled conditions, such as temperature and pressure. The top-down techniques comprise oxidative treatment, microwave treatment, ultrasonication, ball milling, electrochemical techniques, laser ablation, and chemical vapor deposition, whereas the bottom-up techniques include hydrothermal processes, microwave treatment, and ultrasonication [4,6,7].

Coming to the origin, the PL-CNDs were discovered in 2004 by Xu and their research group while developing single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) [8]. In brief, while synthesizing by an arc-discharge method, short and tubular-shaped carbon nanoparticles were observed during the purification of SWCNTs. The obtained nanoparticles were spherical and demonstrated an emission property. Generally, these PL-CNDs are formed by the degradation of carbon precursors under external forces (such as temperature and pressure) followed by aromatization, nucleation, and growth of CNDs [4,9,10]. Interestingly, the emission property of the PL-CNDs can be altered by hetero atom doping and surface passivation procedures. The variations in the PL could be observed due to the increased surface oxygen functionalities, bandgap generation, heteroatom internalization, highly active edges, and cringle-crangle or zig-zag arrangements of CNDs structures [11–13]. These modified PL-CNDs were successively employed in biomedical applications to determine the in vitro cell uptake, in vivo accumulations, biodistribution, bioimaging, and tracking due to their fluorescence nature. Interestingly, these fluorescent nanoparticles could be used as an alternative to organic dyes by considering the enhanced fluorescence nature [14,15]. Similar to PL-CNDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are also demonstrating a luminescence nature. In our previous works, GQDs were developed using graphene oxide by refluxing method [16] and demonstrated their compatibility [17–19]. Therefore, nano-sized luminescence nanoparticles could be easily infused into the micellar drug delivery systems for cellular and biological drug delivery tracking. Thanks to the luminescence property of nanomaterials.

Even though, several publications report the biocompatibility of the nanoparticles in in vitro cell studies, determination of the toxicity for a longer uptake in the in vivo model is also critical. In addition, PL-CNDs are found hemocompatible at low concentrations (0.01 and 0.1 mg/mL) when they were developed from cyclodextrins [20], soot of polystyrene foam waste (60 μg/mL) [21], and sugarcane molasses (0.2–2.0 mg/mL) [22]. A few studies highlight the precursor and synthesis-dependent toxicity variations in PL-CNDs [23]. Even several publications report the photoinduced toxicity and surface passivation-induced toxicity of PL-CNDs due to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [24–31]. On the other hand, the topic is still debatable and the mechanisms are yet to be discussed in detail. A study highlights the irradiation and time-dependent toxicity of PL-CNDs on the Human breast cancer cell line SKBR3 and normal human breast epithelial cell line MCF-12A. The results found that the PL-CNDs developed at lower temperatures by the pyrolysis method exhibit more toxicity in comparison to nanomaterials synthesized at higher temperatures [31]. A similar kind of study shows the cytotoxicity of glucose-derived CDs upon irradiation on normal (HEK-293) and cancerous (HeLa and HepG2) human cells [29]. Therefore, identifying the toxicity of the developed PL-CNDs derived from different approaches is yet to be disclosed via in vitro and in vivo models. Moreover, the results obtained from in vitro studies cannot determine the in vivo performance due to the permeability and cellular barriers. Hence, studying toxicity in both in vitro and in vivo models is warranted.

Nowadays, various organizations and animal welfare committees are minimizing the utility of large animals for laboratory experiments, and suggest to use of artificial intelligence or small animal models for the determination of toxicity, which has brought a paradigm shift in biomedical research. A plethora of literature determined the toxicity of cobalt nanoparticles [32], citrate-capped gold nanoparticles [33], gold nanoparticles [34], single-walled carbon nanotubes [35], multiwalled carbon nanotubes [35], graphene [36], carbon nanosphere, carbon nanofiber [37], candle soot derived carbon nanoparticle [38], fullerene C60, carbon black (CB) [39] etc on a genetic model Drosophila melanogaster (D. melanogaster). The D. melanogaster model has recently become highly favored as a versatile eukaryotic system for investigating the toxicity, genotoxicity, and biological impacts of exposure to nanomaterials [40–46]. It is noteworthy to mention that, PL-CNDs have been widely employed for various biomedical applications, and many reports have not been studied on the D. melanogaster model. Therefore, the present work focuses on the determination of the toxicity of in-house developed egg white derived blue PL-CNDs on in vitro Human Corneal Epithelial (HCE) cells and in vivo D. melanogaster models. To determine the formation of PL-CNDs derived from egg white, various optical and surface morphological characterizations were performed. In vitro and in vivo experiments on HCE cells and the D. melanogaster model suggest the compatible nature of CNDs. The results highlight the utilization of fluorescent nanomaterials for drug delivery and tracing applications. The current work details the preliminary cytotoxic assays on ocular epithelial cells and D. melanogaster model. Further, our goal is to anchor the therapeutic agent to PL-CNDs, followed by determining their permeation in in vivo ocular and neuronal models.

To make carbon nanodots, the albumen was extracted from eggs that were bought from a nearby hatchery store. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), toulene, and hydrochloric acid, were received from Sigma Aldrich, while Spectrum Labs supplied the dialysis membranes (0.5–1 kDa MWCO membrane). A 0.22 μM polyethersulfone (PES) membrane was bought from HiMedia for the filtration. Reagents for cell culture were acquired from Thermo Scientific Limited's Gibco. The remaining reagents were all purchased and utilized exactly as is, without any purification.

The optical characterizations were performed by emission spectroscopy using the F-2700FL SPECTROPHOTOMETER 4J1-0004 equipment and UV–visible spectroscopy using the UV-1800 Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer, Japan. Axis Ultra, Kratos Analytical, UK, equipment was used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy in order to analyze the elemental compositions. FTIR-ATR, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan, was used to record the FT-IR spectral data in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The crystallinity was determined by Bruker Eco D8 Advance. Malvern Zetasizer Ultra was employed for zeta potential and particle size analysis (DLS). Utilizing ZOE, BioRad, and a microplate reader (FluoSTAR Omega, BMG Labtech), the in vitro cell culture analysis was carried out. A confocal microscope (Zeiss LSM 800) was used to take pictures of the brain and the GIT.

Egg white-derived PL-CNDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method [47]. In short, sterile syringes were used to extract 50 mL of egg white from raw eggs. Additionally, the collected egg white was placed inside the stainless-steel autoclave and moved to a 100 mL reactor covered with Teflon. The reactor was securely sealed and maintained for 48 h at 210 °C in a hot air oven. The reactor was allowed to cool once the heating process was finished, and the yellow suspension that resulted was gathered. Oily black material on the autoclave wall's sides was present in the yellow suspension. To separate the carbon dots (CNDs), the entire suspension was taken out, centrifuged, and filtered using a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone (PES) filter membrane. Furthermore, a 0.5–1 kDa molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) membrane was used to dialyze the resulting yellow suspension for 24 h. After removing the organic contaminants from the sample by mixing the dialysate with toluene, the aqueous layer was recovered, concentrated using a rotor evaporator, and then lyophilized. After being examined using optical and surface morphological characterization techniques, the produced CNDs were kept at 4 °C until they were needed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap measurement by Tauc plot method
ce:section.ce:para:
Through the Tauc equation,
α h υ = B
(
h υ − E g
)
n
where hυ is the photon energy, B is a proportionality constant, and n is an exponent that equals 1/2 for direct transitions, the optical band gap, Eg, and absorption coefficient, α [48–50].

Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM): Ham's F12 supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % antibiotic-antimycotic solution was used to culture human corneal epithelial cells (HCE). After three successive passes, they were used for the tests after being incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 and subcultured when they reached 70 % confluence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assessment using Methyl Thiazolyl Tetrazolium (MTT) assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The MTT test was used to assess the cytotoxicity of the compound on HCE cells (2). Cells were seeded at a density of 5000 cells/well in 96-well microtiter plates, and they were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified environment with 5 % CO2 [16,19]. Following the addition of CNDs to the cells at concentrations of 20–250 μg/mL, the cells were incubated for an additional 24 h. After adding 100 μL of MTT solution (1 mg/mL) to each well, the wells were incubated for an additional 4 h. Once formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μL of DMSO (FluoSTAR Omega, BMG Labtech), their absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a multimode microplate reader. As a control, the untreated cell was employed. For the positive control experiment, 0.1 % Triton X-100 was used, and untreated cells were considered the control.
Cell
viability =
(
optical
density
of
the
cells
treated
with
samples
/

)

An in vitro cell morphological investigation was carried out to assess the surface morphology of cells in the presence of varying concentrations of PL-CNDs. HCE cells were seeded into 24- well plates at a density of 10,000 cells per well for the experiment, and they were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 in a humidified environment. After adding CNDs to the cells at concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 μg/mL, the cells were incubated for an additional 24 h. After that, the cells were captured using inverted microscope (Zeiss).

Using an AO/EB staining technique, the various stages of cell apoptosis caused by PL-CNDs were examined. HCE cells were seeded into 24 well plates at a density of 10,000 cells per well for the analysis, and they were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 in a humidified environment. The untreated (control) cells are considered completely viable and have intact morphology. Test substances, such as CND, were introduced to the cells at concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 μg/mL, and they were then incubated for an additional 24 h. Following the designated treatment duration, cells were cleaned with 1X PBS and spent medium from the wells were extracted. Additionally, cells were fixed for 20 min with cold methanol, and then they were stained for 15 min at 37 °C in the dark with acridine orange: ethidium bromide (2 μg/mL, 1:1). The excess stain was eliminated and cells were washed with PBS twice. Cells were overlaid with PBS and imaged under green and red channels of a fluorescence imager (ZOE, BioRad, California, USA).

Wild-type D. melanogaster (Canton-S) strain was used. Flies were reared at 24 ± 1 °C with a relative humidity of 65–70 % and a 12:12 h light-dark cycle on a standard Drosophila creat wheat medium [50].

Feeding Drosophila melanogaster flies with five distinct concentrations of PL-CNDs (50, 100, 150, 200, and 250 μg/mL) was conducted to assess their impact on fly survival. Alongside these experimental groups, a control group receiving standard fly food and a positive stress control group receiving standard fly food supplemented with 1 % H2O2 [51] were included for comparative analysis.

Flies: The wild-type D. melanogaster (Canton-S) flies were provided with three varying concentrations (20, 60, and 100 μg/mL) of PL-CNDs. Different concentrations of PL-CNDs were combined with a 5 % yeast solution and periodically sonicated to achieve a consistent dispersion. This mixture was then placed into fresh food vials and stored at room temperature for 24 h. The following day, 30 female flies were introduced into each vial treated with nanoparticles. These flies were subsequently utilized for survival, climbing, and ROS assays. In all experiments, the control group consisted of flies that were fed solely with 5 % yeast without PL-CNDs. Every 3–4 days, the flies were moved to new vials treated with PL-CNDs to avoid sticking to the previous media [52].

A survival/toxicity assay evaluated the impact of PL-CND exposure across varying concentrations in Drosophila melanogaster [45]. For each condition (control, 1 % H2O2, and PL-CND treatments), 25 adult female flies were housed in individual vials with the respective food source, with each treatment group replicated in triplicate. Mortality was recorded daily for a 20-day period. To minimize the influence of media degradation, flies were transferred to fresh vials containing the corresponding treatment every five days [52]. Flies that escaped or became adhered to the food source were excluded from the analysis. Survival curves, generated using GraphPad Prism 8, were employed to determine statistically significant differences in survival rates between the experimental groups. This experimental paradigm was subsequently applied for climbing and biochemical assays.
Both male and female flies in both the control and treatment groups were monitored for any morphological alterations following 20 days of feeding on nanoparticles.

To assess the effect of PL-CND treatment on motor function in Drosophila melanogaster, a negative geotaxis assay was conducted, adhering to the methodology as outlined [45]. Groups of flies were introduced into vertical glass tubes, each 25 cm in length and sealed at both termini with parafilm perforated to permit adequate gaseous exchange. Following a gentle mechanical stimulus to induce descent, the locomotor capacity of the flies was quantified by recording the number of individuals traversing a 20 cm vertical distance within a 60-s observation period. All experimental conditions were evaluated in triplicate to ensure robustness and statistical validity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS measurements in the brain and GI tract region
ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced in the brain and gastrointestinal tract following PL-CND treatment were assessed using the CM-H2DCFDA (a General Oxidative Stress Indicator). The brain and GI tissues were separated and incubated in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) along with CM-H2DCFDA (5 μM) for 1 h at 25 °C in the absence of light. The samples were rinsed twice with PBS, with a 20-min interval between washes. Subsequently, the samples were imaged with a confocal microscope (Zeiss LSM 800), and the relative fluorescence intensity in both the brain and GI tract regions was quantified [45].

Control and PL-CND treated flies were homogenized in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (PB). Cellular debris was removed by centrifugation of the homogenate at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The resulting supernatant, containing soluble proteins, was collected and utilized for subsequent protein quantification and antioxidant activity assays (Pandey et al., 2014).

The total protein concentration was determined spectrophotometrically using the Bradford assay (Bradford, 1976), with absorbance readings obtained at 595 nm. A standard curve was generated using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the reference protein to quantify the unknown protein samples.

The superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity in fly lysates was quantified using the pyrogallol autoxidation assay, as detailed by Li [53] and Ramasarma et al. [54]. This method leverages the principle that SOD catalyzes the dismutation of superoxide radicals (O2∙−), which are generated during the spontaneous oxidation of pyrogallol. Consequently, the presence of SOD in the lysate inhibits the rate of pyrogallol autoxidation. This inhibition is spectrophotometrically monitored by measuring the decrease in the rate of absorbance increase at 340 nm. The specific SOD activity was then calculated according to the formula previously described [53].

Catalase activity was determined spectrophotometrically by quantifying the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) at 240 nm, following a previously established protocol [55]. The assay involved monitoring the decrease in absorbance at 240 nm at 10-s intervals over a total period of 120 s. Enzyme activity is expressed as micromoles (μmol) of H2O2 consumed per minute per milligram (mg) of total protein.

The Total Antioxidant Capacity (TAC) assay, following the methodology of Prieto et al. [56], quantifies the antioxidant potential of a given sample by evaluating its capacity to impede the reduction of molybdenum (VI) to molybdenum (V). The resultant molybdenum (V) species subsequently forms a green-colored phosphomolybdenum complex. Spectrophotometric analysis at a wavelength of 695 nm allows for the determination of the complex concentration, which exhibits a direct proportionality to the extent of molybdenum (VI) reduction. The TAC value is then extrapolated by referencing the sample's absorbance against a calibration curve constructed with quercetin, a well-characterized antioxidant standard.

The biocompatibility and climbing assay values were determined by calculating the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). A one-way ANOVA analysis of variance (Tukey's multiple comparisons test) was performed for statistical analysis using GraphPad Prism 8 software. Statistical significance was defined based on the following p-values: ns-non-significant, ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, ∗∗∗P < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical spectroscopic and surface morphological studies
ce:section.ce:para: The PL-CNDs were synthesized using egg-white by the hydrothermal method as shown in Fig. 1 . To confirm the properties of the prepared PL-CNDs, we have performed optical and surface morphological characterizations. The UV–visible spectral data demonstrated the broad peak from 260 to 290 nm, which represents the availability of surface functional groups (-COC, CO, etc.) due to the presence of n-π and n-π∗ transitions (Fig. 2 A).

The PL-CNDs showed excitation-dependent behavior from 290 to 450 nm attributed to the presence of surface defects, edge centers, and zig-zag arrangements. The excitation-dependent phenomenon helps to use PL-CNDs for various biological/multicolour imaging applications by exciting the material at different wavelengths (Fig. S1). The sample was excited with a wavelength of 310 nm and the emission peak was recorded at 420 nm (Fig. 2A). The surface of PL-CNDs is decorated with various functional groups (-COC, CO, etc.), which absorb the illuminated energy and emit accordingly, which can be seen in Fig. S1.

It's noteworthy that these surface groups do not emit light independently; however, when they are bound to the surface of CNDs, they share their lone pair of electrons with the π orbitals of the foundational carbon core, functioning as emissive trap sites [57,58]. These groups produce energy levels at various energies within the band gap (discussed in the subsequent sections) of the material. The obtained optical characterizations are completely accordant with the previously reported literature [47]. The PL-CNDs were slightly yellowish under bright light and emitted blue color under UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm. The obtained CNDs were less than 2 nm in size with a zeta potential of −10.43 mV suggesting the occurrence of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on the surface nanoparticle (Fig. 2B) [47]. The HR-TEM image depicts the presence and formation of spherical-shaped PL-CNDs (Fig. 2C). Even, Zhang et al., reported the synthesis of sphericle shaped egg derived PL-CNDs obtained at 220 °C in 48 h hydrothermal synthesis.

Further, the HR-TEM analysis demonstrated the occurrence of particles distributed from 1.4 to 3.4 nm in diameter with the average of 2.1 nm similar to the present work [47]. Further, homogeneous segregation of the PL-CNDs appeared with the interplanar lattice spacing of 0.26 nm representing the (020) diffraction facet of graphite carbon core structure (Fig. 2D) [59]. The disordered and random arrengement of diffraction rings found in the single-area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) demonstrates the crystalline graphite and amorphous phases of PL-CNDs (Fig. 2E) similar to the reported study [60]. Further, the histogram plot determined from HR-TEM analysis suggests the mean particle size of PL-CNDs are approximately 3.2 nm (Fig. 2F).

To further confirm the surface functional groups and elemental composition of the prepared PL-CNDs, X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) analysis was performed. From the XPS survey scan spectrum, we found that the presence of 4 elements such as C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p in the CNDs at the binding energy of 285.09 eV, 400.15 eV, 531.4 eV, and 163.2 eV with the percentages of 78.53, 3.97, 17.37, and 0.13, respectively (Fig. 3 A). Further to understand deeply, deconvoluted high-resolution spectral analyses were performed. From the C1s high-resolution spectra, we have found binding energy peaks at 284.17 eV, 287.58 eV, and 292.31 eV respectively for C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO/COOH functional groups (Fig. 3B). Further, O1s high-resolution spectra confirm the presence of C–O and C–OH functional groups, which are responsible for the peaks that appeared at 531.24 eV and 533.14 eV (Fig. 3C). On the other hand, N1s peaks also appeared at 400.42 eV and 401.18 eV responsible for pyrrolic (=N–H) and graphitic (=N+-H) nitrogen (Fig. 3D). Therefore, the obtained data is completely accordant with the results obtained from UV-spectral analysis. In the reported study, authors characterized the PL-CNDs using XPS studies and found the binding energies for C1s, N1s, and O1s at 285.3, 399.4 and 532.0 eV having the elemental percentages of 46.40, 13.94, and 30.39, including S (0.12 %), respectively [47].

It is noteworthy to mention that, though the PL-CNDs were developed using similar methods, variations in the elemental percentages could be greatly seen. The probable variations could be due to the degree of oxidation and quality of the precursors [61,62]. The degree of oxidation relates to the emission intensity of the PL-CNDs. The potential process for creating carbon cores from the albumen (egg white) precursor can be described as follows: i) Initially, the proteins in the egg white undergo hydrolysis, breaking down into small peptides and amino acids during the early stages of hydrothermal treatment, ii) Some of the amino acids then partially polymerize before being carbonized into CNDs cores that are surrounded by various oligomers, and iii) As the oligomer-coated CND cores increase in size, the outer oligomers gradually diminish and eventually disappear throughout the dehydration and carbonization processes, leading to the development of highly emissive PL-CNDs [47].

The appearance of broad diffraction band centered at about 24.5°, represents the (002) crystal plane of graphite structure demonstrating disordered amorphous structures of CNDs [63] (Fig. 4 A).The results also indicated that, the PL-CNDs are comprised of 27.5 % and 72.5 % of crystalline and amorphous nature. The probable dual nature could be due to the presence of various functional groups on CNDs as reported earlier [64,65]. Thus, the XRD results are well corroborated with the HR-TEM analysis. Further, FT-IR spectral analysis was performed to determine the presence of functional groups on the prepared PL-CNDs (Fig. S2). The peaks that appeared at 3231 cm−1 and 3367 cm−1 are responsible for –OH and –NH stretching vibrations. The shoulder peak that occurred at 2972 cm−1 is associated with –CH stretching vibrations. Further, peaks were identified at 1296 cm−1 for –CH bending vibrations, whereas 1400 cm−1 was spotted as C–N bending vibrations. The CC associated CO peak was noticed at 1634 cm−1 similar to the reported publication [47]. Therefore, multiple peaks appeared in the FT-IR spectra suggesting that, the PL-CND has C, N, and O peaks. The obtained data from FT-IR are in complete accordance with the XPS analysis. Next, the optical band gap of PL-CNDs was calculated by using a Tauc plot derived from UV–visible spectra. The graph shows the appearance of two band gaps Eg = 1.8 eV and Eg = 4.9 eV respectively, which are probably due to the presence of polydispersity of the particles in the synthesized PL-CNDs (Fig. 4B). Similar kind of results were observed when functionalized molybdenum disulfide quantum dots have different particle sizes [50]. Generally, smaller particles possess larger band gaps, whereas bigger have smaller band gaps.

The biocompatibility assay was performed to investigate the effect of as-prepared PL-CNDs on the HCE cell lines (Fig. 5 ). The ocular region is very sensitive among other parts of the human body. In particular, response of HCE cell lines are dependent on both the level of shear stress and exposure time, and these cultures of corneal cells will predict the toxic reactions in the intact cornea in vivo. In addition, toxicity could be determined without the use of animals in the preliminary study due to the phenotypic similarity of animal corneal tissues. Moreover, much reports did not focussed on the determinations of toxicity of photoluminescent nanomaterials on HCE cells except few works [16,19]. Therefore, HCE cell lines were selected for the in vitro cytotoxicity studies.

To perform the studies, 20–250 μg/mL of PL-CNDs were added to HCE cells wherein, more than 90 % cell viability was observed during the treatment of 24 h. Besides, the size of the nanodots also contributes to good cell viability by not causing any cell damage. During the treatment, cellular uptake could happen either by caveolae-mediated and clathrin-mediated pathways from the extrcellular environmet, and still it is unknown. Similar to PL-CNDs, high photoluminescence properties and appreciable cell viability were reported by our group for luminescent graphene quantum dots (20–100 μg/mL) derived from graphene oxide when focusing on drug delivery applications [16,19,66]. In addition, considering concentrations of CNDs [20–22] choosed for cytotoxicity analysis in the reported studies demonstrates similar compatibility trends like the present work. By pointing that, we believe, the CNDs developed from egg-white also possess hemocompatible behavior.

Zhou et al. developed CNDs by using citric acid and ethylenediamine via the hydrothermal method. To identify the cellular uptake pathways, CNDs were treated with PC12 and RSC96 cells for an hour and uptake inhibitors were employed to determine the same. The decreased uptake of CNDs approximately 30 % inhibition rate for methyl-β-cyclodextran (MβCD) treated cells, and an inhibition rate of approximately 20 % for chlorpromazine treated cells, indicates that endocytosis is happening through caveolae and clathrin-mediated pathways [67]. A similar kind of inhibitors were used in another study to determine the internalization of CNDs into NIH/3T3 and L929 Cells [68]. The results indicated that the cell internalization is following energy-dependent uptake-endocytosis pathways. On the other hand, the deciding factor for endocytosis is the size of the nanoparticle [69]. Regarding endocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis depends on the protein clathrin to form coated pits for internalization of molecules (0.5–10 μm) till submicrons, while flask-shaped membrane invasions known as ‘caveolae’ (60–80 nm) allow particles of limited size (<100 nm) [70–72]. A study explaines the energy-independent and energy-dependent pathways to detail the internalization of 20 nm GQDs, and highlighs that, the caveolae-mediated endocytosis may be the main mechanism [73]. In addition to the caveolae pathway, another group found that 50–100 nm SWNTs could be internalized by clathrin-coated vesicles, and that short SWNTs (less than 50 nm) could partially enter cells directly through an energy-independent method [74].

The PL-CNDs used in this study had a particle size of less than 2 nm and were extracted from egg white using a hydrothermal process. Given that particle size is a significant factor, we think that PL-CNDs made from egg whites are internalizing into HCE cells through both energy-dependent and energy-independent manner, either by caveolae and clathrin mediated pathways. However, because PL-CNDs are so small, the caveolae-mediated endocytosis pathway might be more noticeable.

Generally, a live/dead staining assay was performed to determine the live and dead cells (stages of apoptosis) induced by the incubation of testing material (Fig. 6 ). The green stain refers to the viability of the cells and the red stained cells indicate the dead cells. In addition to that, the presence of yellowish-red cells confirms the stages of apoptosis of cells. In the present work, in addition to cytotoxicity analysis, we have performed live/dead staining assays by incubating PL-CNDs for 24 h. It has been noticed that, the concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 μg/mL did not cause the apoptosis of the cells. The untreated cells are considered as control and complete viable. Hence, the PL-CNDs effectively work as a compatible material for biological applications.

The incubation of toxic material alters the cell morphology of the cultured cell lines. Therefore, investigating the possible morphological changes induced by PL-CNDs was demonstrated by using HCE cell lines (Fig. 7 ). Herein, 20, 60, and 100 μg/mL of PL-CNDs were treated to the cell lines and incubated for 24 h and observed the cells under an inverted microscope. It has been noticed that the incubation did not cause any toxic effect on HCE cells, and hence intact morphology was reported for all the tested concentrations. Hence, in vitro cell morphology determination test confirms the biocompatibility of the PL-CNDs derived from egg white towards the sensitive HCE cell lines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo D. melanogaster studies

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the increased utilization of various nanoparticles in industrial sectors, biomedical and other fields, and the subsequent exposure of the nanoparticles to humans, animals, and the environment, it is necessary to investigate the toxic effects elicited by it. Therefore, the survival and climbing assay was conducted as shown in Fig. 8 , to evaluate the concentration-dependent survival rate of the flies.

For the survival assay, adult D. melanogaster flies were exposed to different concentrations of PL-CNDs, and the results were in comparison to control flies. In brief, survival assay in Drosophila melanogaster revealed a dose-dependent toxic effect of PL-CND administration. Over a 20-day period, exposure to PL-CND concentrations ranging from 50 to 250 μg/mL in 2–3 day-old adult flies led to a statistically significant increase in mortality compared to the untreated control group. This observation underscores the inherent toxicity of PL-CNDs in this in vivo model, demonstrating a compromised survival rate in exposed organisms at higher concentrations (Fig. 9 A). However, long exposure showed more than 80 % cell survival, which suggests possible utilization of PL-CNDs for neurotoxicity analysis and drug biodistribution determination applications.

Similar to the current study, Bhat et al., reported the in vivo toxicological effect of onion-peel-derived mesoporous carbon nanospheres (CNS) in vivo D. melanogaster model, wherein nanospheres were synthesized at various temperatures (500–1000 °C). The D. melanogaster survival study for the CNS developed at various temperatures demonstrated a good number of flies survival rates for the concentrations of CNS700, CNS800, CNS900, and CNS1000 from 10 to 100 μg/mL for both male and female flies, similar to our study [52]. It is well known that, at lower temperatures, due to the partial carbonization, nanospheres may not form and could result in only an aggregated structure. This leads to a negative effect on the in vitro and in vivo models by rendering toxicity. In addition, nanoparticles may rupture the cells during internalizations, leading to cell death. Therefore, the nanoparticles/nanospheres developed using high temperatures result in a decrease in the particle size and show good compatibility and non-toxicity to the in vitro cell line and in vivo models. Even, similar kind of results were reported by Lee et al., when they treated D. melanogaster with 100 μg/mL of carbon nanofiber having a diameter ranging from 150 to 200 nm, with a length of 2–15 μm. In addition, at higher concentrations (1000 μg/mL), a higher rate of mortality was reported due to toxic effects [37].

Further, to evaluate the influence of PL-CNDs on the locomotor activity flies, the climbing or negative geotaxis assay was conducted. PL-CND administration in a Drosophila melanogaster model resulted in a significant and dose-related decline in locomotor function, as quantified by a negative geotaxis climbing assay (Fig. 9B). Compared to vehicle controls, PL-CNDs-exposed flies demonstrated a marked impairment in their upward climbing response. These data suggest that PL-CNDs induce a disruption of neurological pathways essential for maintaining normal motor capabilities in Drosophila at higher concentrations. Nonetheless, until 150 μg/mL treatment, Drosophila showed more than 80 % climbing activity, which is necessary for biomedical applications [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS measurement in the brain and GI tract region
ce:section.ce:para: The level of ROS generation in the brain and GI tract regions upon PL-CND treatment was measured using the CM-H2DCFDA method. The results clearly demonstrate that with increase in the concentration of PL-CND, there is an exponential increase ROS level generated (Fig. 10 C and D). However, the ROS generated at 50 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL is not toxic enough to induce mortality in flies as demonstrated by survival (Fig. 10A) and climbing assays (Fig. 10B). These experiments also suggest that higher concentrations of PL-CND will induce toxicity in the in vivo system and concentrations up to 100 μg/mL is safer for biological application or experiments. The data obtained from the D. melanogaster is in accordance with the in vitro cell culture studies.

To understand the activity of PL-CNDs on Drosophila melanogaster model, we evaluated superoxide dismutase (SOD) and Catalase activity, and antioxidant capacity. The dietary administration of PL-CNDs elicited a statistically significant and dose-dependent augmentation of superoxide dismutase (SOD) enzyme activity in Drosophila melanogaster. Quantitative analysis revealed that Drosophila populations reared on food media supplemented with varying concentrations of PL-CNDs demonstrated a marked increase in SOD activity when compared to a control group maintained on standard, un-supplemented media (Fig. 11 A). Further, dietary supplementation of Drosophila melanogaster with PL-CNDs elicited a dose-dependent augmentation of Catalase (CAT) enzymatic activity. As depicted in Fig. 11B, CAT activity in flies exposed to PL-CND-supplemented food was significantly elevated compared to control groups. In addition to the above, the impact of PL-CNDs on total antioxidant capacity (TAC) was investigated in Drosophila melanogaster. Adult flies were maintained on food media supplemented with varying concentrations of PL-CNDs, and subsequent TAC analysis revealed a positive correlation between PL-CNDs concentration and TAC levels. Specifically, as depicted in Fig. 11C, a dose-dependent augmentation of TAC was observed in the PL-CNDs-treated groups relative to the untreated control cohort.

In conclusion, we synthesized and characterized the photoluminescent carbon nanodots (PL-CNDs) followed by analyzing their in vitro and in vivo toxicity potential on HCE cells and the Drosophila melanogaster model. The obtained spherical in-shaped PL-CNDs exhibited the emission at 420 nm, having a particle size of 2 nm with a zeta potential of −10.43 mV. Further, various in vitro biological studies, such as MTT, AO/EB, and bright field imaging studies, suggest that PL-CNDs are compatible. The cell survival and climbing assay points to the in vivo cytocompatibility of PL-CNDs on the D. melanogaster model. The analysis of dissected gastrointestinal tract (GIT) and brain fluorescence analysis suggests the permeation of PL-CNDs on 10 days of exposure. In Drosophila melanogaster, dietary supplementation with PL-CNDs elicits a dose-dependent enhancement of antioxidant defense mechanisms. This is evidenced by the increased activity of key antioxidant enzymes, SOD and CAT, and the elevation of total antioxidant capacity. Therefore, the study opens up new avenues to utilize the PL-CNDs for in vitro and in vivo fluorescence bioimaging with tracking abilities in drug conjugates, particularly in neurological disorders.
